specification.topic-statement: The framebuffer serialization system converts raw terminal framebuffer bytes into a structured JSON representation that encodes text content and styling metadata.

scope.in-scope: Parsing ANSI escape sequences from framebuffer bytes, extracting text and style information, and producing a JSON document describing rows, spans, colors, and cursor state.
scope.boundary: This topic receives a byte buffer containing raw terminal output (text intermixed with ANSI escape sequences), terminal dimensions, and cursor state. It does not produce or maintain the framebuffer.
This topic produces a JSON string. What consumes that string is outside this topic's scope.

field.name: text
field.note: content segment sharing a single style

field.name: spans
field.note: always at least one span per line

behavior.entry-validation: If the framebuffer byte pointer is null, the operation fails immediately and returns an error. No output is produced.
behavior.initialization: Before parsing begins, a line array is allocated with one slot per terminal row. Each slot is initialized with its row index, an empty span array (initial capacity four), and a fresh text buffer (initial capacity 256 bytes). A single empty initial span is prepared in a transient working area for accumulating text during the parse.
behavior.parsing-framebuffer-bytes:
The parser iterates through every byte in the framebuffer, advancing one character at a time unless an escape sequence or multi-byte UTF-8 character causes a larger jump.
When the cursor-hide escape sequence (ESC[?25l) is encountered, it is skipped entirely and contributes no text or style change.
When the cursor-home escape sequence (ESC[H) is encountered, it is skipped entirely and contributes no text or style change.
Any other byte pair matching ESC followed by '[' triggers escape sequence handling. The parser inspects the bytes immediately following the '[' to determine which sequence applies.
A sequence of the form ESC[38;5;NNNm (where NNN is a decimal integer) sets the foreground color to the parsed index value. Before the color is applied, any accumulated text in the current span is flushed to the current line. The new color takes effect for subsequent text. If the byte sequence does not match the expected prefix "38;5;" exactly or there are insufficient bytes remaining, the sequence is skipped.
A sequence ending in 'm' preceded by '0' resets all style flags. Any accumulated text in the current span is flushed to the current line. The foreground color, bold, dim, and reverse flags are all cleared to their zero/false defaults.
A sequence matching the bold attribute sets the bold flag. Any accumulated text in the current span is flushed before the flag is updated.
A sequence matching the dim attribute sets the dim flag. Any accumulated text in the current span is flushed before the flag is updated.
A sequence matching the reverse attribute sets the reverse flag. Any accumulated text in the current span is flushed before the flag is updated.
Any escape sequence that does not match the recognized patterns is skipped. The parser advances past the ESC[ prefix and then continues advancing until it reaches a byte value in the printable ASCII range (0x20 to 0x7E inclusive), at which point it stops (without consuming that byte). This ensures unknown sequences do not produce text output.
When a CR+LF pair is encountered, the current line is finalized. If the current span contains any accumulated text (or if the current line has no spans yet), the span is flushed to the current line. The parser then advances to the next line. The current row index increments by one.
The flush condition for CRLF checks either text_len > 0 OR span_count == 0. This ensures that even a line with no text produces at least one flush, which combined with the post-processing step guarantees every line has at least one span in the output.
Any byte that is not part of an escape sequence or CRLF is appended to the current span's text buffer. If the text buffer is full, it is doubled in capacity before the character is added.
behavior.post-processing: After the parse loop completes, every line in the line array is checked. If a line has zero spans, an empty span is allocated and added to it. This guarantees that every line in the output has at least one span, even if the line received no text during parsing.
behavior.span-flushing:
Flushing moves the current working span into the line's span array. If the accumulated text buffer is empty, the span is discarded without being added. If adding the span would exceed the line's span array capacity, the capacity is doubled. After flushing, a new 256-byte text buffer is allocated for the next span, and the new span inherits the current style.
If the current span has no text, flush is a no-op. No empty span is added to the line during normal text flushing (only the post-processing step adds empty spans to lines with zero spans).
behavior.json-construction:
After parsing and post-processing, the output JSON document is constructed from the collected lines.
The root JSON object contains the fields: type (the literal string "framebuffer"), rows, cols, cursor_row, cursor_col, cursor_visible, and lines. The lines value is a JSON array.
Each line is serialized as a JSON object with fields row (integer) and spans (JSON array). Lines are comma-separated within the array.
Each span is serialized as a JSON object with fields text and style. Text content is JSON-escaped (double quotes and backslashes are escaped with a preceding backslash). Style is a JSON object.
The style JSON object contains only non-default fields. A foreground color is included only if it is non-zero. Bold, dim, and reverse are included only if true. If all style attributes are at their defaults, the style object is empty. Fields within the style object are comma-separated and appear in the order: fg, bold, dim, reverse.
behavior.memory-management: All intermediate allocations (line arrays, span arrays, text buffers) are made under a temporary context that is freed after the final JSON string is produced. The JSON output string is allocated under the caller-provided context and outlives the intermediate allocations.
